content.text: Empty Array (Run Once)
Passing an empty array
as the second argument to
useEffect

tells React that this effect has no dependencies. React runs the effect exactly once — after the initial mount — and never re-runs it for subsequent renders. This is the correct pattern for one-time setup: service workers, analytics initialisation, third-party library instantiation, or any work that needs to happen once when the component first appears.

attrs.alt: Dependency array comparison: no array vs empty array vs [dep1, dep2]

content.text: The Three Forms of the Dependency Argument
The second argument to
useEffect
controls when the effect re-runs. There are three distinct forms, each with different semantics:
The empty array in form 2 is not an optimisation hint — it is an exact contract: React will never re-run this effect as long as the component is mounted.
How React Compares Dependencies
React stores the dependency array from the previous render. On each re-render, it compares each element with
Object.is
(strict equality). With an empty array, there are no elements to compare, so React always considers the deps unchanged — the effect never re-fires.
Common Use Cases for Empty Dependency Array
The empty dependency array is the right choice when the effect must run once and the work it does is not parameterised by any current props or state.
Service Worker Registration
Analytics Initialisation
Third-Party Library Setup
Global Event Listeners
Cleanup in Mount-Only Effects
Even a mount-only effect should return a cleanup if it acquires a resource. The cleanup runs on unmount, preventing leaks when the component is removed from the tree.
Strict Mode and the Double Mount
React Strict Mode intentionally mounts, unmounts, and remounts every component in development to help you find cleanup issues. This means your
effect fires
twice
in development strict mode: once on the initial mount, once after the forced remount. In production it fires once. If your effect behaves differently the second time, its cleanup is incomplete.
Strict Mode is a feature, not a bug:
if your effect breaks when run twice, it will also break in production when React's concurrent features remount components for performance. Fix the cleanup, not the strict mode check.
The Stale Closure Trap
The most common mistake with
is trapping a prop or state value in a stale closure. The effect captures the value from the first render and never updates, even if the prop changes on every subsequent render.
If you want the effect to react to
name
changes, add it to the dependency array. The empty array is correct only when the effect truly does not depend on any reactive value.
Using a Ref to Read Latest Values Without Re-Running

When you genuinely want an effect to run once but still read the latest value of something, store that value in a ref. The ref is mutable, so the effect always reads the current value without triggering a re-run.
This pattern (sometimes called an
effect event
) is common in libraries that need stable subscriptions but want to call the latest version of a user-provided callback without re-subscribing.
ESLint and the exhaustive-deps Rule
The ESLint rule
react-hooks/exhaustive-deps

warns when you use a variable inside an effect but omit it from the dependency array. An empty array with variables inside is a likely bug — the effect will forever see stale values from the initial render.
Trust the lint warning. An empty dependency array that silences a lint warning with a
// eslint-disable
comment is almost always a bug waiting to appear. The correct fix is to restructure the code so the effect genuinely has no reactive dependencies, or to declare the dependencies properly.
When Not to Use an Empty Dependency Array
Scenario
Correct deps
Why
Fetch data for a specific user ID prop
[userId]
Effect must re-run when userId changes
Subscribe to a channel prop
[channel]
Need to switch subscriptions as channel changes
Log a page view when route changes
[route]
Should fire on each navigation, not just once
Set document title from a prop
[title]
Must update whenever title prop changes
One-time analytics init (no deps used)
Correct — no reactive values inside
Register service worker (no deps used)
Correct — purely setup code, no deps
SSR Compatibility of Empty-Array Effects
An empty-array effect still has a timing consideration with server-side rendering. On the server,
useEffect
never runs — the callback is completely skipped during SSR. On the client, the effect fires after hydration completes and the first paint is done.
This means you can safely use browser-only APIs (
window
localStorage
, WebSockets) inside a
effect without any server-compatibility guard — they will never be called server-side.
Per-Instance vs Module-Level Initialisation

Because the empty-array effect runs once per component instance — not once globally — each mounted copy of a component gets its own independent setup. This is different from module-level initialisation, which runs once for the entire application.
If you want a single shared instance across all mounts, the module-level pattern is correct. If each component instance needs its own resource, the empty-array effect is the right tool.
Custom Hooks with Mount-Only Effects

One of the best uses of the empty dependency array is inside custom hooks that encapsulate one-time setup. The calling component does not need to think about dependency arrays at all — the hook owns the lifecycle.
Note that the hook above treats
url
as stable. If the caller may pass a different URL, the hook should accept the URL as a dependency and use
[url]
instead of
so the connection is recreated when the URL changes.
Testing Components with Empty-Array Effects
Testing mount-only effects requires triggering the mount. With React Testing Library, simply rendering the component triggers the mount and the effect. Use
waitFor
or
act
to flush async work.

The second test confirms the defining property of the empty array: the effect does not re-run even when the component re-renders. This is the invariant worth testing when correctness depends on single-run behaviour.
Summary
Passing
as the dependency array causes a
useEffect
to run exactly once, after the initial mount. React never re-runs it because there are no dependencies that can change.
useEffect(() => { ... }, []) runs after the first paint and never again.
React compares deps with Object.is — empty array means nothing to compare, always skipped.
Use [] for one-time setup: service workers, analytics init, global event listeners, third-party library creation.
Always return a cleanup function if the effect opens a resource, even with [].
Strict Mode fires the effect twice in development — use this to verify cleanup correctness.
A stale closure is the main hazard: variables captured at mount never update. Use a ref or add proper deps.
Trust ESLint exhaustive-deps warnings — an unexplained empty array with variables inside is usually a bug.
